The stars were not scattered jewels but a single, glowing river of silver that flowed silently through the center of the heavens. This river, the Aethelgard, was guarded by a jealous serpent named Vesper. Vesper coiled around the world, his scales absorbing all light, ensuring the night remained his domain.

One evening, a small moth named Luna, drawn not to flame but to the cool shimmer of the Aethelgard, dared to fly too close. She did not wish to steal the light, only to dance within it. Vesper, sensing the intrusion, snapped his great jaws, intending to swallow her whole. But Luna was swift; she fluttered erratically, her wings dusting the surface of the silver river.

In his fury, Vesper struck the river with his massive tail, shattering the smooth flow. The Aethelgard exploded into a billion fragments. Luna, caught in the blast, was coated in the glowing dust and ascended high above, becoming the pale, watchful orb we see today. The shattered river pieces scattered across the dark obsidian, becoming the stars, forever out of the serpent’s reach, guiding travelers through Vesper’s shadow.

Long ago, before voices could bounce off stone, the mountains were silent giants, hoarding every sound that dared to touch their peaks. The wind would scream, but the mountains would swallow the noise instantly. This silence was the domain of Orestes, the Stone King, who despised the chaotic chatter of the living world.

One day, a young lark named Lyra, known for the sweetest song in creation, flew too high. She breached the cloud line and sang a melody of such piercing joy that it cracked the Stone King’s composure. Enraged by the intrusion, Orestes snatched Lyra from the sky and imprisoned her within a cavern of black basalt, intending to silence her forever.

But Lyra’s spirit was made of sound, not flesh. Though trapped in the dark, she refused to stop singing. She sang of the sun she missed and the rivers she loved. The basalt walls, usually so greedy, became saturated with her music. They could hold no more. Finally, the stone shattered, not outward, but inward. The mountains could no longer hoard sound; they were forced to release it. Now, whenever a voice calls out to the peaks, the spirit of Lyra throws it back, ensuring that the Stone King can never again keep the world silent.

Myth: In the earliest days, the oceans were not restless; they were a flat, stagnant pane of glass, ruled by the silent leviathan, Thalossos. He coveted stillness above all else, swallowing even the smallest ripple before it could spread. The world’s waters were a mirror that reflected nothing but the empty sky, for Thalossos forbade movement.

One day, a tiny, vibrant flying fish named Coralia, whose scales shimmered with the colors of the dawn, dared to breach the surface. She did not just swim; she leaped, arching through the air in a display of pure, kinetic joy. The splash she made upon re-entry was a thunderclap in the silent world. Thalossos roared from the depths, furious at the disturbance, and summoned a whirlpool to drag Coralia down into the abyss.

But Coralia’s leap had sparked a memory in the water itself—a memory of flow and rhythm. As she was pulled down, her shimmering scales rubbed against the currents, igniting a chain reaction. The water refused to be still. It began to heave and crash, mimicking her leap. Thalossos could no longer hold the ocean flat. The waves were born that day, forever rising and falling in an eternal attempt to catch the flying fish that taught them to dance.
